dissection.title: Characterisation of the peritoneal mesothelium and its role in the formation of post-surgical adhesions.

constructs.evidence: a key mesothelial marker is the transcription factor Wilms’ tumour protein 1 (Wt1)
Qualitative analysis of additional markers (cytokeratin, podoplanin) was also carried out
Qualitative analysis of additional markers (cytokeratin, podoplanin) was also carried out
the presence of a cell population expressing Wt1 and the mesenchymal marker platelet-derived growth factor receptor α (PDGFRα) either alone or in combination was identified

design.detail: utilised to demonstrate that Wt1-positive mesothelial cells tagged with GFP prior to surgery developed mesenchymal characteristics and were present in adhesions
design.evidence: a mouse adhesion model was utilised to demonstrate that Wt1-positive mesothelial cells tagged with GFP prior to surgery developed mesenchymal characteristics and were present in adhesions

design.detail: used to understand the role of Wt1 in the murine peritoneum
design.evidence: The second aim, addressed in the third results chapter, was to understand the role of Wt1 in the murine peritoneum via a lineage tracing model

sample.detail: used for characterisation of the healthy mesothelium
sample.evidence: The first aim was to characterise the healthy mesothelium (including the submesothelium) of wild type mice

measures.detail: used for characterisation of intestinal and abdominal wall sections
measures.evidence: The first aim was to characterise the healthy mesothelium (including the submesothelium) of wild type mice, via immunofluorescence of intestinal and abdominal wall sections

measures.detail: used for analysis of tissue samples
measures.evidence: ScRNA-seq analysis of tissue samples collected from mice that had undergone surgery to induce adhesion formation, or a sham surgery was carried out

analysis.detail: revealed a significant difference in expression between the intestinal and abdominal wall mesothelium
analysis.evidence: quantification of Wt1 expression was carried out and has revealed a significant difference in expression between the intestinal and abdominal wall mesothelium

analysis.detail: used for analysis of additional markers
analysis.evidence: Qualitative analysis of additional markers (cytokeratin, podoplanin) was also carried out

data.detail: collected from mice that had undergone surgery to induce adhesion formation, or a sham surgery
data.evidence: ScRNA-seq analysis of tissue samples collected from mice that had undergone surgery to induce adhesion formation, or a sham surgery was carried out

findings.detail: between the intestinal and abdominal wall mesothelium
findings.evidence: quantification of Wt1 expression was carried out and has revealed a significant difference in expression between the intestinal and abdominal wall mesothelium
Qualitative analysis of additional markers (cytokeratin, podoplanin) was also carried out to establish that heterogeneity is also seen in other mesothelial markers

findings.text: changes to the gross peritoneal anatomy
findings.evidence: multi organ failure occurred in ablation mice with a range of phenotype severities being observed

contributions.detail: provides a platform for a comparison of adhesion and control tissue to be carried out
contributions.evidence: A complete characterisation of the healthy mesothelium allows a better understanding of the changes that occur during mesothelial pathologies, which allows for the identification of potential targets to ameliorate adhesion formation or reduce the severity

limitations.detail: to complete the analysis of tissue samples
limitations.evidence: ScRNA-seq analysis of tissue samples collected from mice that had undergone surgery to induce adhesion formation, or a sham surgery was carried out, however this revealed that mesothelial cells were being lost during the preparation process, and optimisation is required to complete this

future.detail: to compare samples from adhesion and control tissue
future.evidence: Repeating scRNA-seq analysis to compare samples from adhesion and control tissue would be beneficial following optimisation
